title: Countries by income classification
subtitle: The World Bank's income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.
caption:
Countries by income classification, World

The World Bank’s income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.

From 1987 to 2024, the number of countries in each World Bank income group shifts visibly across four stacked layers, measured in count along the vertical axis ranging from 0 to 250. The top green layer, labeled “High income,” begins near 40 countries in 1987, rises to about 65 by 1990, and remains relatively flat through 2024, hovering just above 60. Below it, the light green “Upper-middle income” band starts around 30 countries, climbs to roughly 40 by 1993, then gradually declines to about 35 by 2024. The pink “Lower-middle income” layer begins near 50 countries, peaks at approximately 65 around 1994, then falls steadily to about 50 by 2024. The bottom purple “Low income” band starts at 50 countries, rises slightly to 65 by 1995, then drops sharply after 2005 to settle near 25 by 2024. The total height of all layers combined stays between 165 and 220 countries throughout the period, with the highest point occurring around 2015. The note clarifies that countries are grouped based on the income classification for each respective year, meaning group membership can change over time. The most visible structural shift is the contraction of the low-income category after 2005, coinciding with expansion in the upper-middle and high-income bands during earlier decades. The data shows a redistribution of countries across income tiers rather than uniform growth or decline in any single group.
